verdict: 2026-07-13 08:01:01 PDT — The server deletion cascade is clean and appropriately deletion-only: the store, crypto, app-context identity, user-data registry, routes, and dedicated tests disappear together without disturbing OAuth, health, logging, headers, or static serving; no dependency change is needed. The overall approach is not merge-ready, however, because the public legal copy still asserts encrypted account storage, multiple live technical documents still describe the removed architecture, and the reconciled logging claims overstate what the unchanged `/api/log` sink guarantees.

findings.title: Terms still promise the removed encrypted account store
findings.claim: The changed public Terms still say saved configuration is retrieved through the Zoom account and that configuration is stored encrypted and tied to that account. That directly contradicts the session-only implementation, the new Privacy Policy, and AC6. This is an active legal-contract inconsistency, not harmless historical context.
findings.alternative: Rewrite the responsibilities and privacy clauses to say configuration exists only for the current browser/meeting session, is not tied to the Zoom account, and is not persisted server-side; retain the aggregate-overlay and operational-data disclosures.
findings.win: Removes two false public contractual promises and makes Terms and Privacy describe the same product.

findings.title: The live documentation inventory remains structurally unreconciled
findings.claim: Several files changed by this story still present the deleted system as current. The architecture SVG says names/rates are sent to an AES-GCM file on a persistent volume, depicts per-person inputs and participant-count SDK access, and labels configuration as encrypted server storage. The canonical opportunity-cost note still documents `rateTable`, overrides, `rateStore`, and cross-device encrypted persistence. The roadmap adds a superseding banner but continues to label the removed store, identity, endpoints, scopes, and paid-product sequence as verified/current. Readers cannot reliably distinguish present architecture from obsolete plans.
findings.alternative: Treat current-state documents declaratively: fully rewrite the architecture SVG and canonical opportunity-cost note around the single-rate session model. Move the old persistence roadmap into an explicitly historical/archive document, or replace its current-state tables and execution plan rather than relying on a banner that contradicts hundreds of subsequent lines. Add a repository-wide current-claim scan as the reconciliation gate, excluding immutable review records explicitly.
findings.win: Eliminates multiple competing sources of truth and leaves one dependable architecture, schema, scope, and roadmap inventory for future work and marketplace review.

findings.title: PII-free logging claims exceed the unchanged sink’s guarantees
findings.claim: The public policy calls operational telemetry the only recorded server data, says it excludes participant personal data, and describes only errors and diagnostics. The retained server also writes `[server] METHOD path` request lines, while `/api/log` accepts any public JSON body up to 100 KB and serializes it verbatim without a server-side schema. Client error payloads include error text, stacks, paths, and user-agent data. Source-side minimization reduces risk, but it cannot justify unconditional claims that the resulting Railway logs are PII-free or contain no arbitrary payloads.
findings.alternative: For this deletion-only story, narrow the claims: disclose both request-line and client-diagnostic logs; enumerate the intended fields, including error text/stack, path, and user agent; say they are minimized and not intentionally populated with presenter configuration rather than guaranteed PII-free; retain concrete retention/access and rights-request language. If a PII-free invariant is required, separately add authentication/rate limiting and a strict server-side event schema before making that claim.
findings.win: Removes a false security/privacy invariant without expanding this teardown’s code scope, while preserving an accurate account of the server-side data that still exists.
